nativeRange.habitat: Reef walls, overhangs, cave entrances, and shaded ledges with strong tidal currents; partial to fully retracted during daylight, fully extended at night to filter feed

decorPreferences: areas of strong non-laminar or alternating flow
position away from direct intense lighting

diet.notes: Entirely non-photosynthetic (azooxanthellate) — contains no zooxanthellae and derives zero energy from light. Survival depends entirely on continuous filter feeding. Diet should consist of live or high-quality preserved phytoplankton (Nannochloropsis, Tetraselmis, Isochrysis; 3–20 µm particle size), supplemented with copepod nauplii, rotifers, marine snow, and other micro-zooplankton. Live phytoplankton is generally considered more effective than preserved formulations. Food must be available almost constantly in the water column — pulse-dosing several times daily at minimum; dedicated NPS dosing systems or refugium-based live cultures are commonly recommended. Baby brine shrimp and cyclop-eeze have also been used as supplemental foods. Regular addition of iodine, strontium, and other trace elements is generally advised.

goodWith: gorgonians and other NPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals with sweeper tentacles
crinoids (known associates that may stress the colony)

summary: Dendronephthya are spectacular azooxanthellate soft corals requiring no light but continuous phytoplankton feeding. Among the most difficult corals in the hobby; most specimens perish within months in typical reef systems.
careNotes:
IMPORTANT: Dendronephthya is widely considered one of the most difficult corals ever offered in the marine aquarium hobby. Captive mortality is extremely high — most specimens collected from the wild decline and die within weeks to months, even under expert care. Purchase of wild specimens is strongly discouraged unless the keeper has a fully dedicated non-photosynthetic (NPS) system and has already successfully maintained simpler NPS species such as Scleronephthya.

Non-photosynthetic biology: Unlike the vast majority of reef aquarium corals, Dendronephthya contains no zooxanthellae. Lighting plays no role in nutrition — high-intensity lighting is unnecessary and may be harmful. These corals tend to open more fully in lower-light conditions and are most active at night.

Feeding: The single most critical and commonly fatal failure point in captive husbandry. Food must be present in the water column nearly continuously, not dosed once per day. Recommended approach involves live phytoplankton cultures (Nannochloropsis, Tetraselmis, Isochrysis), target-fed several times daily, supplemented with rotifers and copepod nauplii. Some sources report water densities of 5,000–50,000 phytoplankton cells per cubic milliliter as effective. Pulse dosing of liquid coral foods alone has generally proven insufficient for long-term survival.

Flow: Alternating, non-laminar (bidirectional tidal-type) moderate-to-strong flow is generally considered optimal, mimicking natural reef surge. Optimal flow velocities have been cited around 15–20 cm/s. Direct, constant laminar flow may damage polyps. Strong flow is essential for food delivery and waste removal — polyps cannot adequately feed without adequate water movement.

Lighting: Low (50–150 PAR). High light is neither required nor beneficial; placing the colony in a shaded area, under an overhang, or in a lower-flow shadow zone of the rockwork is generally advised. High-intensity lighting may cause tissue retraction or stress.

System maturity: A fully cycled, mature reef system (minimum 12 months) with established live rock, diverse microfauna, and stable water chemistry is generally considered a prerequisite. Some researchers have suggested that unknown nutritional contributions from fish waste, sponge metabolites, or complex reef community byproducts may be important, which would explain why bare/new systems fail even when target foods are supplied.

Water quality: Pristine, highly stable parameters are required. Protein skimming, RO/DI top-off, and regular partial water changes help maintain necessary water quality. Regular supplementation with iodine, strontium, and other trace elements is generally recommended.

Sizing and growth: In the wild, colonies can reach 1–2 meters in height. Under rare successful captive conditions, growth rates of 30 cm in a few months have been documented. Captive colonies are typically sold as small frags (10–20 cm) attached to rubble or live rock.

Dedicated NPS system: Expert opinion consistently holds that Dendronephthya should only be attempted in a purpose-built NPS (non-photosynthetic) reef tank. Co-housing with photosynthetic corals requiring high PAR creates an inherent conflict between the lighting needs of other corals and the low-light preference of Dendronephthya. A dedicated NPS tank with dimmed or no lighting, continuous phytoplankton dosing infrastructure, and mature live rock is the approach most likely to succeed.

Color variants: Available in brilliant reds, oranges, pinks, purples, and yellows. Red and orange forms marketed as 'Strawberry Coral' are the most commonly traded. Color is derived from calcareous sclerites embedded in the tissue, not from photopigments — coloration does not fade due to bleaching the way zooxanthellate corals do, but fades with declining health.

Species note: Dendronephthya is a large genus of 250+ described species; the aquarium trade commonly sells multiple species under the umbrella name 'Carnation Coral' or 'Tree Coral'. D. klunzingeri and D. rubeola are among the most frequently discussed species. Husbandry requirements are generally identical across species in the genus.
breedingNotes: Reproduction in captivity has not been documented. Sexual reproduction occurs in the wild; asexual fragmentation is theoretically possible but is not practiced in the aquarium trade due to the extreme baseline difficulty of keeping this genus alive. Fragging is not recommended.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn – Carnation Corals in the Home Aquarium
additional.notes: Detailed NPS husbandry article. Key data: optimal flow 15–20 cm/s bidirectional laminar; phytoplankton diet 5,000–50,000 cells/mL; colony height up to 1 m with foot/month growth rates documented; live phyto preferred over preserved; bacterial infection implicated as common cause of death. Cross-checked feeding methodology and flow requirements.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Carnation Tree Coral Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–78°F / 22–25.5°C), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, care level expert, flow strong, placement anywhere, feeding multiple daily. Native range: South Pacific. Good agreement with LiveAquaria on all key parameters.

additional.name: Reeflings – Dendronephthya klunzingeri Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked lighting 50–100 PAR, temperature 24–26°C, salinity 1.023–1.026, placement in shaded areas. Minor disagreement on temperature upper bound (26°C vs 25.5°C from other sources — averaged to 26°C max). Minor disagreement on PAR range — ReefChasers listed 100–150 PAR while Reeflings listed 50–100; final range of 50–150 adopted.

additional.name: Subdiversion – Dendronephthya spp. species profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Indian Ocean, Red Sea, Pacific), depth range 3–45 m, colony size 1–2 m, taxonomy (Family Nephtheidae), nocturnal filter-feeding behavior. Confirmed natural predators include Dermatobranchus and Armina nudibranchs, coral crabs, spider crabs, cowries, and crinoids.

additional.name: Reef Builders – NPS Coral Tank Size Considerations
additional.notes: Expert NPS practitioner recommends ~50 gallons (~200 L) with large sump as practical minimum for NPS reef systems; nano setups possible but require extreme maintenance regimens. Adopted 200 L as minimum tank volume.
